summary: Comprehensive risk-based regulation of artificial intelligence systems. Classifies AI by risk tier: unacceptable (banned), high-risk (strict obligations), limited-risk (transparency), minimal-risk (free use). Bans social scoring and real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces. Requires conformity assessments, human oversight, and transparency for high-risk AI. General-purpose AI models face additional obligations. Fines up to 35 million EUR or 7% of global turnover.

Gold-standard data protection law. Six lawful bases for processing (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interests). Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection, automated decision-making. Data minimisation and purpose limitation principles. 72-hour breach notification to supervisory authority. Data Protection Officers required for large-scale processing. Fines up to 20 million EUR or 4% of global annual turnover.
keywords: data protection,privacy,personal data,consent,right to erasure,data breach,GDPR,data subject,data controller,data processor

summary: UK implementation of GDPR principles supplemented with derogations for law enforcement and intelligence services. Retained as UK GDPR post-Brexit. Establishes Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) with enforcement powers. Includes provisions for journalistic, academic, and artistic exemptions. Special categories of sensitive data receive enhanced protection.
keywords: data protection,privacy,personal data,UK GDPR,information commissioner,ICO,sensitive data,consent

summary: India's first comprehensive data protection law. Data fiduciaries must obtain free, specific, informed, and unconditional consent. Data principals have rights to correction, erasure, grievance redressal, and nomination. Significant data fiduciaries face enhanced obligations including data protection officers and audits. Cross-border data transfer by government whitelisting of jurisdictions. Penalties up to INR 250 crore for breaches. Children's data requires verifiable parental consent.

California's comprehensive privacy law granting consumers: right to know what data is collected, right to delete, right to opt-out of sale of personal information, right to non-discrimination. CPRA 2020 amendment added right to correct, right to limit sensitive personal information use, and created California Privacy Protection Agency. Applies to businesses meeting revenue or data volume thresholds.

South Africa's data protection law based on eight processing conditions: accountability, processing limitation, purpose specification, further processing limitation, information quality, openness, security safeguards, data subject participation. Establishes Information Regulator SA. Mandatory breach notification. Operators (processors) must be contracted. Prohibition on processing of special personal information without consent or exemption.
keywords: data protection,privacy,personal information,information officer,South Africa,POPIA,information regulator,processing conditions

summary: Nigeria's comprehensive data protection legislation replacing the 2019 Regulation. Establishes Nigeria Data Protection Commission (NDPC) as the regulatory body. Lawful basis requirements, data subject rights (access, correction, deletion, portability). Mandatory breach notification. Cross-border transfer safeguards requiring adequacy or safeguards. Data Protection Compliance Organisations (DPCOs) as licensed compliance service providers.
keywords: data protection,privacy,Nigeria,personal data,data commission,NDPC,breach notification,data subject rights

summary: Ghana's data protection framework requiring registration of all data controllers with the Data Protection Commission. Eight data protection principles parallel to UK DPA. Data subjects have rights of access and correction. Sensitive personal data (health, politics, religion, criminal) receives enhanced protection. Cross-border transfers require Commission approval unless adequate protection exists.

law_name: PIPEDA — Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act
summary: Canada's federal private-sector privacy law applying to commercial activities. Ten Fair Information Principles: accountability, identifying purposes, consent, limiting collection, limiting use/disclosure/retention, accuracy, safeguards, openness, individual access, challenging compliance. Mandatory breach-of-security notification to Privacy Commissioner and affected individuals. Being modernised by proposed Bill C-27 (Consumer Privacy Protection Act).

law_name: Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados — LGPD 2018
summary: Brazil's GDPR-equivalent data protection law. Ten legal bases for processing including consent, legitimate interest, and public policy. Data subject rights: confirmation, access, correction, anonymisation, portability, deletion, information about sharing, and revocation of consent. Data Protection Officer (DPO) requirement. National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) as regulator. Extraterritorial application for data collected in Brazil.

Singapore's data protection law establishing nine data protection obligations: consent, purpose limitation, notification, access and correction, accuracy, protection, retention limitation, transfer limitation, openness. Do Not Call (DNC) registry for telemarketing. Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) as regulator. 2020 amendment added mandatory breach notification, data portability, and right to correction.

Regulates online platforms and intermediary services. Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) with 45M+ EU users face systemic risk assessments and independent audits. Bans targeted advertising to minors and based on sensitive categories. Mandates algorithm transparency and recommender system disclosures. Prohibits deceptive interfaces (dark patterns). Requires rapid removal of illegal content. Ofcom-equivalent enforcement at national level. Fines up to 6% global turnover.
keywords: online platforms,digital services,content moderation,algorithms,targeted advertising,EU,dark patterns,misinformation,intermediary liability

summary: Comprehensive UK law placing duties of care on online platforms. Ofcom as regulator with powers to fine up to 10% global turnover or block access. Illegal harms duties apply to all services. Child safety duties require risk assessments and age verification for harmful content. Category 1 services (largest platforms) face transparency and accountability duties. Safety by design principle embedded. Criminalises cyberflashing, epilepsy trolling, and sharing of non-consensual intimate images.
keywords: online safety,content moderation,children,age verification,UK,harmful content,Ofcom,duty of care,platform regulation

law_name: NIS2 Directive — Network and Information Security 2022
summary: EU cybersecurity directive expanding the scope of critical infrastructure sectors subject to security obligations. Covers energy, transport, banking, health, water, digital infrastructure, public administration, and space. Entities must implement technical and organisational security measures. Mandatory incident reporting: initial notification within 24 hours, full report within 72 hours. Supply chain security requirements. National CSIRTs (Computer Security Incident Response Teams) required. Member states must transpose by October 2024.

Foundation cybersecurity legislation creating three tiers of criminal offence: unauthorised access to computer material (up to 2 years), unauthorised access with intent to commit further offences (up to 10 years), unauthorised acts with intent to impair computer operation (up to 10 years). Extraterritorial jurisdiction where any link to UK exists. Widely cited as a template in Commonwealth jurisdictions. Kenya's computer crime legislation frequently mirrors its definitions of unauthorised access and modification.
